sutta: Thus have I heard on a certain occasion the exalted one was staying near Savatthi at Jetta grove in Anathapindika spa.
commentry: All the suttas start with these words thus have I heard this is the buddhist tradition decided at the first sangha council the exalted one is a translation of Bhagavā Bhagavā is one of the ten names of the Buddha this monastery was donated by Anathapindika.

sutta: Monks, I know of no other single form by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a woman a woman's form obsesses a man's heart.
Monks, I know of no other single sound by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by the voice of a woman a woman's voice obsesses a man's heart.
Monks, I know of no other single scent by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a woman.
Monks, I know of no other single taste by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a woman.
Monks, I know of no other single touch by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a woman.
Monks, I know of no other single form by which a woman's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a man a man's form obsesses a woman's heart.
Monks, I know of no other single sound, smell, taste, touch by which a woman's heart is so enslaved as it is by the voice of a man a man's voice obsesses a woman's heart.
commentry: These five are the objects of the five senses they are the objects of sensual desire the opposite sex is the most powerful among them this is why chastity is required in spiritual paths many monks disrobe due to sensual desire Buddha warned monks about proximity to nuns in another sutta Ānanda asked how to deal with women Buddha said do not look if you must look do not speak if you must speak do so with mindfulness monks must depend on lay people so cannot isolate completely this creates danger like deer near a farmer farmer is a simile for Māra only strong samādhi protects the monk without higher pleasure of pīti and sukha one remains attached to sensual pleasure.

sutta:
Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of sensual desire if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its development and increase as a feature of beauty or attractiveness in him who pays not thorough attention to the feature of beauty sensual desire if not already arisen arises or if already arisen is liable to develop and increase.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of ill will if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its development and increase as a repulsive feature in him who pays not thorough attention to the repulsive feature ill will if not already arisen arises or if arisen it is liable to develop and increase.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of sloth and torpor if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its development and increase as regret drowsiness languor fatigue after meals and stupidity of mind in him who is of torpid mind sloth and torpor if not already arisen arises and if arisen is liable to develop and increase.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of restlessness and remorse if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its development and increase as non tranquility of mind in him who is of troubled mind restlessness and remorse arise or if arisen is liable to develop and increase.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of doubt and wavering if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its development and increase as unthorough attention in him who gives not thorough attention doubt and wavering arise or if arisen are liable to develop and increase.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to prevent the arising of sensual desire if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its abandonment as the feature of ugliness in him who gives thorough attention sensual desire does not arise or if arisen it is abandoned.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to prevent the arising of ill will if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its abandonment as goodwill released by the mind in him who gives thorough attention to goodwill ill will does not arise or if arisen it is abandoned.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to prevent the arising of sloth and torpor if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its abandonment as effort exertion striving in him who energetically strives sloth and torpor do not arise or if arisen it is abandoned.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to prevent the arising of restlessness and remorse if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its abandonment as tranquility of mind in the tranquil minded restlessness and remorse do not arise or if arisen it is abandoned.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to prevent the arising of doubt and wavering if not already arisen or if arisen to cause its abandonment as thorough attention in him who gives thorough attention doubt and wavering do not arise or if arisen it is abandoned.
commentry:
So here the first sutta is saying that sensual desire arises mainly because of the feature of attractiveness or beauty for example a man may be attracted to a woman because he thinks that that woman is beautiful or attractive in some way and similarly a woman may be attracted to a man because she finds something attractive in his looks now this feature of beauty or attractiveness what is it it is a perception it is a perception that is formed in our mind and if we look into some other suttas later we find that it is one of the things that we associate with the self there are five things the Buddha calls them the panchakanda five aggregates that we associate with the self first one is the body the second one is feeling third one is perception then volition then consciousness so the Buddha says in some other sutta that perception is formed from habit because of the way nature has made beings we have certain perceptions but the Buddha said perceptions can change for example if a person does not cultivate his mind he might find the form of the opposite sex very attractive but somebody who develops his mind cultivates the 32 parts of the body then he begins to see the body as it really is not only the external part of the body but the internal part he will be able to picture a person in terms of blood and bones and flesh and fat and urine and saliva then he might find that the body is no more attractive so perceptions can be changed perceptions are something that we form arbitrarily for example one person may find someone handsome another may not so it is in the eyes of the beholder so if we pay thorough attention we see that it is just an opinion in the mind and it does not enslave us so much. Here the Buddha says ill will increases because there is something repulsive that we do not like and that makes anger develop but if we pay thorough attention to that repulsiveness then ill will can decrease so in both cases thorough attention is important.

Sloth and torpor are due to regret drowsiness eating too much and a dull mind.

Most people have a non tranquil mind tranquility must be developed people find it hard to stay alone they become restless and want to go out this shows restlessness is natural without training.

Yoniso manasikara means thorough attention yoni means source manasikara means work of the mind it is attention that brings you back to the source of the problem.

If attracted to someone as beautiful look at the unattractive aspects then desire diminishes.

Mettā is goodwill it is the antidote to anger true mettā requires strong concentration with strong jhāna it can be radiated properly.

To fight sloth we need effort too much sleep increases sloth the more you sleep the more you want.

The antidote is samatha tranquility must be cultivated.

Doubt is resolved through reflection we must examine and test teachings only after seeing truth do we accept.

sutta: Monks, I know not of any other single thing so uncontrollable as the uncultivated mind the uncultivated mind is indeed a thing uncontrollable.
Monks, I know not of any other single thing so controllable as the cultivated mind the cultivated mind is indeed a controllable thing.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing so conducive to great loss as the uncultivated mind the uncultivated mind indeed conduces to create loss monks I know not of any other single thing so conducive to great profit as the cultivated mind the cultivated mind indeed conduces to great profit.
commentry: I stopped here for a while to comment so if the mind is not cultivated now it is very difficult to control it in fact when we practice meditation especially samatha meditation we are exercising control over the mind every moment when the mind tries to run away we bring it back to one single object it runs away again because that is the tendency of the mind to run and keep on running and we bring it back to one object so every moment we are exercising control over the mind and if we keep on doing that then when samādhi arises we are able to control the mind samādhi is the result of controlling the mind so when we have samādhi then the mind is controlled already and that is why the Buddha says there is no other single thing so controllable as a cultivated mind.

sutta: Monks, I know not of any other single thing that brings such suffering as the mind that is uncultivated not made much of the mind that is uncultivated not made much of indeed brings great suffering monks I know not of any other single thing that brings such joy as the mind that is cultivated made much of the mind that is cultivated made much of indeed brings great joy.
commentry: So you see somebody who has a lot of suffering usually you find that person he has not cultivated his mind now suffering there are two types of suffering one is mental suffering one is physical suffering mental suffering is mostly man made and the Buddha says the difference between an ariya and a putu jhāna an ordinary person is that an ariya has no mental suffering he accepts things as they come he has no mental suffering he does not proliferate whatever problems he has he does not think about them and make them multiply in his mind and so he has no mental suffering so if we develop our mind cultivate our mind we do not have that kind of unnecessary suffering but there are some types of physical suffering that we cannot run away from for example getting old getting sick and dying that we cannot run away from but mental suffering most of it we can avoid if we cultivate our mind.

sutta: But suppose monks a pool of water lucid tranquil and unstead then the man who has eyes to see while standing on the bank could see the oysters and the shells the pebbles and the gravel as they lie and the shoals of fish that dart about why so because of the untroubled nature of the water monks just so it is possible for that monk of whom I speak with his untroubled mind to understand either his own profit or that of others both his own profit and that of others it is possible for him to realize states surpassing those of ordinary men the excellence of truly ariyan knowledge and insight what is the cause of that the untroubled nature of his mind.
commentry: Monks okay now I have to comment on this this troubled state of the mind and the untroubled state of the mind refers to the five hindrances just now we heard that the uncultivated mind is the troubled mind in some other sutta the Buddha gave a very clear simile for the five hindrances suppose a man tries to look for his reflection in water a person with strong sensual desire is like water with dye the water is no longer clear so he cannot see his reflection for ill will the simile is boiling water the water is so disturbed you cannot see your reflection for sloth and torpor it is like water covered with weeds you cannot see through it for restlessness and worry it is like water stirred by wind ripples and waves prevent seeing clearly for doubt it is like muddy water placed in a dark place you cannot see anything at all so here the Buddha is saying the turbid state of water is like the troubled mind a mind full of hindrances cannot see clearly clear still water is like a cultivated mind a tranquil mind with strong samādhi clear bright and still.

sutta: Monks, I know not of any other single thing so quick to change as the mind in so much as it is no easy thing to illustrate how quick to change it is that's all.
commentry: This sutta is telling us that the mind changes extremely fast because it changes extremely fast we should use it to good advantage for example if we have a good intention that comes up like thinking maybe I should become a monk because it is a good intention we should quickly act if we do not act the mind will change the mind is fickle so when there is a good intention we act quickly on the other hand if there is a bad intention we should not act on it for example anger arises if we act on anger we will do something unwholesome because the mind is troubled so instead of acting we do nothing we wait for the anger to subside because the mind changes quickly it will pass so we just wait and do not act this is using the changing nature of mind with wisdom.

sutta: That mind monks is luminous but it is cleansed of things that come from without.
commentry: These are the two suttas that I am going to explain first these suttas are very interesting when someone is new to the Dhamma they may not notice the importance the Buddha said this mind is luminous this mind is Citta there are three words Citta vinyana and mano vinyana is consciousness mano is the thinking faculty Citta is a broader aspect of mind some say they are the same some say different aspects here Citta is luminous but defiled by things from outside these are superimposed defilements this sutta is interesting because of two views of nirvana some say all consciousness stops completely others say something remains there is a sutta where the Buddha says there is still a light the mind is luminous in the kevatta sutta the Buddha speaks of non manifestative consciousness boundless luminous consciousness this is different from the six sense consciousness this is unsupported consciousness it does not depend on objects this view is controversial hindu concept of atman is sometimes compared but their concept is not the same as buddhist atta they also reject ego and say it must be extinguished Buddha also uses the term brahma body in aggañña sutta the Buddha says tathagata is Dhammakaya brahma kaya so the ultimate state is described as brahma body this is similar in wording to hindu brahma the meaning of this sutta is that the mind is originally bright but defilements from outside darken it ordinary people see darkness when eyes are closed arahants experience brightness thai forest teachers speak of original mind as bright defilements arise through six sense consciousness energy is scattered through senses this causes the mind to become dark through samādhi and jhāna the mind becomes one pointed energy is no longer scattered the mind regains luminosity jhāna is mental brightness this sutta supports the view of original luminosity but we must be careful about claims of existence or non existence in nirvana the Buddha said do not say it exists or does not exist it is beyond the mind it can be experienced but not known by ordinary mind.

sutta: The Buddha said monks if for just the lasting of a finger snap the monk indulges the thought of mettā here translated as good will such a one is to be called among not empty of result is his jhāna he abides doing the master's bidding he is one who takes good advice and he eats the country's arms food to some purpose what then should I say of those who make much of such a thought.
commentry: Here the Buddha is trying to say that even good will or loving-kindness or impersonal love universal love if you even have uh a partner of mettā then that manga is really is called a real monk and here the Buddha also say it's not empty of result it is jhāna so from here it would appear that to practice mettā you have to attain janafus to attain jhāna then only you can radiate thismettāl now.

sutta: 0.6 Of slight account monks is the loss of such things as relatives miserable indeed among losses is the loss of wisdom and then.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing that conduces to the confusion to the disappearance of true dharma as does negligence negligence indeed conduces to the confusion and disappearance of true dharma then.

The Buddha said monks those monks who point out what is not dharma as Dhamma such conduct of theirs is to the loss of many folk to the misery of many folk to the loss the injury the misery of devas and mankind moreover such beget great demerit and caused the disappearance of this true dharma uh that's that's the suta.
commentry:
These two suttas also tell us that if we want to preserve the true dharma if we want the dharma to last then we should not be negligent if we look around buddhism I very often seem to be very negligent we don't take our religion very seriously and because we don't take our religion seriously it conduces to the disappearance when we compare ourselves with other religions we find it's very shameful other religions is you seldom find for example a christian who does not have a bible who does not read his bible regularly or a muslim his quran whereas for uh buddhism there are so many so-called buddhists in this country we ask them what is the basic Buddha's teaching what what are the Buddha's discourses they can't tell you and if we count we ask how many buddhists have the nikayas the suttas the discourses of the Buddha that they should study I look up every day then you find so few of our buddhists have the holy books of the Buddha and that is not good for our religion that's why if we are serious that if we treasure the Buddha's teachings and we want to preserve it for our future generations we should take the trouble to teach our young look at the muslims they uh they get their children every day to go to class agama perhaps spend two hours as I have seen in certain areas in kl after going to school they finish at about one o'clock they go back take their lunch and then by three o'clock they go to the house of gamma and they finish at five o'clock every day the children will go for the class akama whereas for our buddhists to ask parents to send their children just once a week on a sunday to go to a sunday sunday school many parents are reluctant and then I think it's very shameful we don't value enough the Buddha's teachings if we value enough we should take our religion seriously as the Buddha has already won here I have seen some for example my own relatives and some of them are catholics and they have a very good practice every night after dinner they get the whole family together after dinner say and they spend one hour where they pray for the good for the peace of the world for the good of the world and they read from their bible so I don't see why our buddhists cannot do the same thing that we are the families that should get together maybe do five minutes or ten minutes of chanting and read something from the suttas for 15 minutes or something and then maybe meditate for 15 minutes and so you you very easily you can spend one hour after dinner every night and that will help to preserve the Buddha's teachings which to us is invaluable now we come to.

Here the Buddha is saying that there are some monks who do not understand the dharma enough so that what is not dharma a Dhamma what is contradictory to the Buddha's teachings they say it is the Buddha's teachings and because of that now they create they create great the merit and they cause the disappearance of the true dharma now that's why it is very important for monks as to study the teachings of the Buddha which the Buddha calls in the suttas as Dhamma Vinaya as I pointed out before the Buddha never taught the tripitaka the Buddha taught the Dhamma Vinaya as he had said in the suttas the word tipitaka or tripitaka was never spoken by the Buddha the Buddha only said Dhamma Vinaya was taught by him and the Dhamma in the suttas in the anguttara nikaya book of the force chapter of the force the Buddha says that Dhamma is the suttas are the Buddha's teachings so because some monks are not very familiar with the suttas what is not dharma what is contradictory to the Dhamma they say is Dhamma and so because of that they cause they help to cause the disappearance of this true dharma one point eleven point one the Buddha said those monks who point out what is not dharma as not dharma such conduct of theirs is to the prophet of many folk to the happiness of many folk to the good prophet and happiness of devas and mankind moreover such monks beget great merit and establish this true dharma now this sutta here the Buddha is saying that among I should point out now what is not dharma as not dharma because in that case they will profit many many people and also they would help to preserve this true Dhamma that is why if we know that something that is being taught now is not according to the Dhamma we should speak out to preserve the true dharma this is not being controversial but just following the advice of the Buddha in fact the Buddha also during his time used to speak out against a lot of wrong teachings that were prevalent during his time and because of that he was also reviled by a lot of these external teachers and in one sutta he said I do not quarrel with the world he said the world quarrels with me one who teaches the Dhamma the true dharma does not quarrel with the world so here we would be following the Buddha's footsteps if we try to preserve the true dharma by speaking out against what is not dharma then.

sutta: 1. 13.7 Buddha said monks I know not of any other single person who so perfectly keeps rolling the supreme wheel of Dhamma set rolling by the Tathāgata as does Sāriputta is the one who perfectly keeps rolling the supreme wheel of Dhamma set rolling by the Tathāgata.
commentry: As you all probably know the Tathāgata is another word for the Buddha the Tathāgata means the dust corn one this sutta I choose to mention it not because it has some a lot of dharma content but because there are some books in them in the mahayana sutras that seem to say the opposite thing for example in the lotus sutra they try to say that the Buddha that the Sāriputta being and arahan did not understand the teachings of the Buddha and instead they tried to show that all the bodhisattvas understood the real teachings of the Buddha whereas.

sutta: Chief among those of great wisdom is Sāriputta chief among those of supernormal powers is the great Moggallāna chief among those who are clairvoyant is anuruddha chief among those who receive offerings is Sīvalī chief among those of retentive memory is Ānanda chief among those who know the Vinaya is Upāli chief among the nuns of long standing is maha Pajāpati Gotamī chief among the nuns of great wisdom is Khemā chief among the nuns of supernormal powers is Uppalavaṇṇā chief among the nuns proficient in Vinaya is Paṭācārā chief among lay followers who are alms givers is anathapindika chief among lay followers who are Dhamma teachers is Citta chief among lay women followers who minister to the sangha is Visākhā megara's mother chief among lay women who nurse the sick is supiya.
commentry: In this 1.14 There are many the disciples of the Buddha who are mentioned the monks and nuns as well as lay men and lay women and eminent ones are mentioned those who are known for something so I won't mention all of them i'll just mention a few of them among the monks the Buddha always praises Sāriputta in theravada in mahayana sutras Sāriputta is sometimes portrayed as having shallow wisdom this is seen in the vimalakirti sutra anuruddha had the heavenly eye and could see the whole world system clearly Sīvalī is known for receiving many offerings people keep his image hoping for merit Ānanda had the best memory and recited the discourses after the Buddha's passing Upāli was foremost in Vinaya discipline maha Pajāpati Gotamī was the Buddha's stepmother she nursed the Buddha after his birth she later became the first nun after Ānanda interceded Khemā was formerly a queen and became foremost in wisdom among nuns Uppalavaṇṇā had great psychic powers and could visit the Buddha even at midnight arahants were allowed to see the Buddha directly Paṭācārā had a tragic life and became an arahant after meeting the Buddha anathapindika built a monastery and supported monks daily Visākhā also supported thousands of monks Citta was a lay follower who attained high states and could teach monks Visākhā was called megara's mother after teaching her father in law supiya once cut flesh from her own thigh to feed a sick monk the Buddha healed her wound instantly.

sutta: The Buddha said it is impossible monks it cannot come to pass that in one world system at one at the end and the same time there should arise two arahants who are fully enlightened once sama sam Buddha but monks it is quite possible for a single arahant a fully enlightened one to arise commentry: these these few suttas here concerning the impossible are also found in majima nikaya number and this particular sutta saying that you cannot find two Buddhas sama sambudas at the same time in one world system is another one of those suttas that seem to be in conflict with the mahayana sutras for example in the earth store bodhisattva they say that many Buddhas came when the Buddha started to preach in fact you can find the diamond sutra in the mahayana sutras also contradicting uh this uh straw bodhisattva sutra because in the diamond sutra it is said that after a Buddha has passed into mahaparinibana if you expect to see the Buddha again or hear his voice or touch him etc then the diamond sutra says that you don't understand the Dhamma and you have gone in along the deviant path in other words once a Buddha has passed into nibana you can never see him again you can never touch him again hear his voice etc so there's one thing if you uh if you investigate you find that there are certain sutras that contradict each other it is only in the original four earliest suttas in the pali suitors and the four nikayas that you find they are very consistent that there is no contradiction one sutta:point one point fifteen point twelve the Buddha said it is impossible monks it cannot come to pass that a woman should be an arahant who is a samasan Buddha but monks it is quite possible for a man to be one and then uh sudan number 13 14 15 and 16 and the commentry: Buddha says it is impossible it cannot come to pass that a woman should be a universal monarch or so here we find these few suttas there are five things that a woman cannot become become a samasan Buddha cannot become a universal king who rules over the whole world cannot become cannot become Māra or satan cannot become brahma and this again you find there's a little contradiction in the lotus sutra where it is mentioned that a woman can become masama sambuda here also it shows that there is a little difference between a man and a woman sometimes we like to think that a man and a woman are the same but there is some difference one point.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing so apt to cause the arising of wrong view if not yet arisen or the increase of wrong view if already arisen as antara consideration or antara attention in him who gives not terror consideration wrong view if not a reason does arise or if already arisen does increase then.

Monks, I know not of any other single thing so app when body breaks up after death to cause the rebirth of beings in the ways the woeful planes the downfall in hell as wrong view possess of wrong view monks beings are reborn in the ways the woeful planes the downfall in hell then I know not of any other single thing so ab when body breaks up after death to cause the rebirth of beings in the happy lord in the heaven world as right view possess a right view amongst beings beings are reborn in the happy lord in the heaven world so here commentr: we can see why wrong view and right view the the the result of having wrong view and the result of having right view and that's why it's so important to have right view because if we have wrong v1 it brings us into the woeful plane so to have right viewer we have first to have to to to have thorough consideration concentration of mind the other thing is we should uh if we have the good fortune to be able to listen to the sadhama the true dharma then which can which can bring us to to have right view and then we are very very fortunate it is uh if we look into the suttas the Buddha said now he contemplated the last 91 kappas 91 ages of time one age of time one world cycle is extremely long the Buddha said it is so long a time that it is quite hard to imagine how long it is in fact the Buddha says to give you an idea of how long a world cycle is the Buddha said you imagine a cube a rock which is seven miles yojana which sometimes translates said to be seven miles or sometimes ten miles seven miles uh long seven miles wide and seven miles high a cube seven miles left and you one once in a hundred years now you take a soft piece of cloth you wipe it once and after 100 years you wipe it once again how long will it take and before that you can wear away that rock just by wiping it with a soft cloth the time is so long that is quite unimaginable and the Buddha said he he looked into the past for 91 world cycles and he only found six Buddhas you know six Buddhas which means that one Buddha appears in more than 10 world cycles on the average life so to be able to listen to the true dharma now is something uh very very fortunate for us sutta: monks one person born into the world is born to the loss of many folk to the discomfort of many folk to the loss discomfort and sorrow of devas and mankind what person one who has wrong view he of wrong of distorted view leads many folk astray from the from righteousness or the right path and plants them in unrighteousness or the wrong path this monks one person born into the world is born for the profit of many folk for the happiness of many folk for the profit comfort and happiness of devas and mankind what person one who has right view he who has right view leads many folk from the rock from the wrong path and plus them in the right path this is the one commentry: so you see if you follow somebody with the wrong view then that is very unfortunate and this person who has wrong view he is born to cause a lot of pain to a lot of people but a person who is born with right viewer he benefits many many people.

Monks, I know not of any other single person fraught with such loss to many folk such discomfort to many folk with such loss discomfort and sorrow to devas and mankind as ma kali that infatuated men just as monks at the river mouth one sets a fish trap to the discomfort suffering distress and destruction of many fish even so makali the infatuated infatuated man was born into the world meetings to be a man trapped for the discomfort suffering distress and destruction of many beings in this uttar commentry: the Buddha did not hesitate to speak badly on makali gosala putta one who had wrong view because he was leading many folk into the woeful planes and sometimes people think that the Buddha was such a nice man that he cannot criticize another person it's not true the Buddha is willing to benefit many people and speak out now even even uh to name somebody who was leading some people to have wrong views we were at.
The Buddha said monks just as even a trifling bit of dung has an evil smell so likewise do I not favor existing even for a trifling time not even for the lasting of a finger snap and.
commentry: The Buddha says that he does not want to exist even for a trifling time and existence to him is likened to dung which has a bad smell so that's how he views existence why does the Buddha say that existence is uh so um uh so obnoxious and sort of so foul like uh is because even Buddhas have to suffer because there are two types of suffering one is mental suffering the other one is physical suffering the Buddha said that all ariyas have done away with mental suffering but they still have physical suffering and we cannot run away from physical suffering as long as we have a body because with a body we have uh aging sickening and dying and the Buddha said in more than one sutta and when he was old that his his body was breaking up and it gave him a lot of suffering so even Buddhas have to suffer and some people are of the opinion that because Buddhas and arahants don't have self that they do not suffer but if that is the case then you can think for example if the Buddha had physical suffering from the body then if there was no if it did not affect him there would have been no necessity for him to enter into a deep janaza or cessation of perception and feeling um which the Buddha did when the pain of the body was quite unbearable he said he had to enter into deep states of meditation so that he did not experience pain in the body and then after that he comes out of it for some time there is no pain but later the pain might come back again so there's one sutta in the itty but it's 2.44 There the Buddha said what because is the nirvana element with residue left here a biku is an arahant one whose asavas are destroyed the holy life fulfilled who has done what had to be done laid down the burden attained the goal destroyed the fetters of being and is completely released through final knowledge however his five sense faculties remain unimpaired by which he still experiences what is agreeable and disagreeable and he feels pleasure and pain sukha and dukkha it is the extinction of attachment head and delusion in him that is called the nibana element with residue left now what because is the nibana element with no residue left then he explains the other part so here the Buddha is saying because arahanna still has the five sense faculties he feels suka and dukkha so that explains why uh the Buddha says that existence uh he he doesn't want to exist uh even for a short time if he if he uh if he could and this uh you'll find is quite uh a different from some later books we see that the Buddha sort of come back again and again or he's always in existence now the next sutta is.

sutta: The Buddha said even as monks in this rose apple land that is Jambudīpa trifling in number are the pleasant parks the pleasant groves the pleasant grounds and lakes while more numerous are the steep precipitous places unfoldable rivers dense tickets of stakes and thorns and inaccessible mountains just so few in number are those beings that welcome when they hear it the Dhamma Vinaya set forth by a Tathāgata more numerous are they that do not.
commentry: Here the Buddha is saying that in india the accessible places where people can use are very much less than those places which are inaccessible like uh steep places mountains uh dense uh forest and jungle unfoldable rivers etc you must remember this sutta was spoken two thousand five hundred years ago so the number uh the the amount of inaccessible places was much more during that time than now and the Buddha said that few people and just like the the the the amount of places that are accessible so uh small amount compared to the whole of india so in the same way few people uh out of the many one to listen to Dhamma Vinaya Dhamma Vinaya is teaches us the truth or reality about existence and it is actually painful to hear the reality of existence and sometimes people don't want to face the truth they want to face their reality they would like to believe what they want to believe what they like to believe so because of that few people want to listen to the Dhamma and the disciplinary code taught by the Buddha the Vinaya so in other words we don't expect to have many followers of the Buddha's teaching in fact most Buddhas when they are enlightened do not want to teach just like our Buddha Buddha after he was enlightened he contemplated the minds of living beings and he found that few people are inclined to listen to the Dhamma and even if they listen few uh even fewer want to practice it so he actually wanted to be a pachika Buddha but later he was persuaded by maha this uh brahma sahampati to turn the wheel of the Dhamma and he did surah we find this happens more more than once that sometimes the Buddha is not inclined to do something um somebody appeals to him to change his mind sometimes they appear once he doesn't want to change his mind then they appeal a second time still he doesn't want to change his mind but when they appeal the third time out of compassion he will change his mind and do something that at first he was not inclined to do so this shows uh that actually pachika Buddhas are able to teach but it's just that they are not inclined to teach it's not like some of the later books I say that particular Buddhas are unable to teach because whatever path that they have walked through is very clear to them the path that they have walked through so it shouldn't be uh difficult at all for them to teach and they would rather teach actually by example they show a good example of being a renuncian and they practice and perhaps people if people ask them in individually of the dharma they might teach but they are not inclined to teach to the whole world and turn the dharma wheel and some people think that that is selfishness but it is not selfishness because it appears to me that every being has its own time and the best teacher is suffering because when we suffer enough and it will bring us to walk the path if we have not been in samsara we are not suffered enough we are not spiritually mature even if the Buddha is around we would not be inclined to follow his teaching so that was that is why we find that during the Buddha's time there were many people who did not want to follow the Buddha's teachings even though the Buddha was in the world now the next is.

sutta: The Buddha says just as monks in this Jambudīpa rose apple land that is india trifling in number are the pleasant parks the pleasant groves the pleasant grounds and lakes while more numerous other steep precipitous places unfordable rivers dense thickets of stakes and thorns and inaccessible mountains just so few in number are those beings who diseasing as men are reborn among men more numerous are those beings who diseasing as men are reborn in hell who are reborn in the wombs of animals who are reborn in the realm of ghosts just so few in number are those beings who deceasing as men are reborn among the devas more numerous are those beings who deceasing as men are reborn in hell in the wombs of animals in the realm of ghosts few in number are those beings who diseasing as devas are reborn as devas or are reborn as men more numerous are those beings who deceasing as devas are born into the three woeful planes of hell the animal realm and the realm of ghosts similarly few in number are those beings who deceasing from hell or from the animal realm or from the ghost realm are reborn as men or as devas more numerous are those beings who deceasing from hell from the animal realm from the ghost realm are reborn back into the hell realm or in the animal realm or in the ghost realm.
commentry: So basically what this is saying is that most men after they pass away they are not they don't come back into the human realm or go up into the deva realm instead most of them go into the warfoot plains the three woeful planes hell animal realm and the ghost realm similarly devas most of the devas when they pass away are reborn into the woeful plains few of them are reborn backed as devas or as human beings and even the beings from the woeful planes after they pass away most of them are reborn back into the woefu planes and not into the human realm or the heavenly realm now what amount we born it back into the woefu planes for example human beings uh I would give a a very rough estimate perhaps out of every 10 people out of every 10 human beings maybe only two or three yeah come back into the human realm human world or are reborn as devas maybe seven or eight out of ten people go down into the whole food plains so from there we can realize that it is quite uh dangerous uh and uh we should not be like most people most ordinary people now they live a very ordinary life and then they go into the uh woeful planes because they follow their inclinations they follow all their lower instincts it would appear like the number of beings in the in the in the world every world system is like a pyramid you know the higher you go the less beings there are the lower you go the more beings there are the base is very wide the top is very narrow now the other thing here you can see here is that then the woefu planes mentioned here and throughout the suttas are only three you know that is the first one the lowest one is the health realm and slightly above that is the animal realm and and better than the animal realm according to samsuta is the realm of ghosts so there are only three planes of existence in the woefu planes later books added another plane called the asura plane but when we check with the suttas we find that it contradicts the sutta because in the sutta two suttas in the diga nikaya one says that the lowest type of asuras are the kalakanja asuras and then later in another sutta it is stated that the kala kanja asuras are also devas so you can see here that if the lowest kind of asuras are devas then all of the asuras must be devasta so there are only three woeful planes and not four.

sutta: The Buddha said truthfully monks these are to be reckoned as gains forest dwelling living on alms wearing rag robes wearing three robes only teaching the Dhamma mastery of the Vinaya wide knowledge the rank of an elder thera the blessing of true development the blessing of a following the blessing of a large following being a man of good family a beautiful complexion pleasant speech to be content with little and freedom from sickness.
commentry: So here the Buddha is saying these things are really worth gaining these are good things to have to practice I would give you a lot of blessing the first one is forest dwelling this is basically for mangala the word forest is actually a translation of the word I think arania aranya is basically a secluded place a quiet place and usually the Buddha mentions a place like a cave up in the fall up in the hills um very quiet secluded place basically because if a monk wants to practice well it is best to to to live in such places the second one also for monks is living on arms that means to go on arms round this is a very good practice for monks because it is not something easy to do sometimes you go on arms around people are quite rude to you they just ask you to shovel or something like that and or they just ignore you or they give you a roots there or something and uh it's uh very good for our ego if we want to wear away our ego these are some of the things which are very helpful that's why it's not easy to be among some people they don't realize that and they I don't appreciate monks enough and that's a loss to them third one is wearing rag ropes rag ropes here is a translation for uh this rope this this the meaning of it now is that it is picked up a cloth that is picked up during the Buddha's time according to the later books uh something like uh 20 or 25 years the first 20 or 25 years of the Buddha's ministry ministry uh he and his disciples only wore uh robes that were made from uh a cloth that was picked up and they would go to these what they call channel grounds cemeteries places where dead bodies were thrown and um the cloth that was left over and the mouse would go and pick it up or from whatever place the cloth was thrown away they would pick it up and sow them into a rope but later the lay disciples of the Buddha asked the Buddha for permission to give ropes to the monks and out of compassion for them to give people a chance to cultivate marina the Buddha allowed uh lay people to make offerings to uh monks uh sometimes uh ap some people don't uh don't realize that it is uh the good fortune to be able to give to monks and uh that is uh that is the the Buddha's so this about red robes and the third one the fourth one is wearing three ropes only the ropes of a manga are generally stated to be three one is the sabung or one sometimes it's called the sarong and then this rope that we are wearing there's a shivarra shivon and then there's another one that's two-layer double lid that is for use as a blanket now then teaching Dhamma teaching Dhamma is also a good thing the Buddha encouraged the teaching of Dhamma that's why the Buddha you find during the Buddha's time and up to now that the Buddha encouraged his monks to teach the Dhamma and not all monks taught Dhamma and not all monks taught meditation there were some monks who were meditation monks and some monks who were dharma preachers and some monks who mastered both and during the Buddha's time there were many types of monks and the Buddha allowed all of them to do what was they what they thought was best for them and the mastery of the Vinaya uh another one also concerning monks is mastery of the rules and the disciplinary rules and if they could live by the disciplinary code that would accumulate they would accumulate a lot of blessings the next one is white knowledge white knowledge is a translation for bahu sacha that means a lot of knowledge of the dharma the Buddha said now very few people can teach the very few people can teach the Dhamma according to the the real uh essence the real Dhamma that the Buddha taught then the next one is the rank of an elder an elder here is a monk is one who has 10 vasas and above a man who has 10 vasas and above is a man who can ordain new monks among who has 10 vassals and above can accept disciples the Buddha did not allow a younger monk to accept disciples but the Buddha asked the monks to practice for the first 10 years to get a lot of experience then after that only to accept disciples that's why uh to be an elder is a blessing the blessing of true department that means a man who can conduct himself well and that is uh a good blessing then the blessing of a following that means uh if a monk can have a following of disciples that that is a blessing um this following can be lay disciples or among disciples and during the Buddha's time we find that several monks they had a large following of among disciples and nowadays we find that not many monks are able to have a following of among disciples some of them have a following of lay disciples but to be had to have a following of among disciples it is very good especially for the religion because there is always a shortage of monks the blessing of a large following to have a a big following of lay disciples and among disciples during the Buddha's time he sometimes he would walk around with 1250 monks and you go from place to place and that would be really impressive and the Buddha why did the Buddha take such a large following with him it was not that I mean it's really very difficult to look after such a large amount of monks and to travel in the group it's a lot of it's like an army moving logistics involved to look after their food and all that but uh that is is it's not easy to have a large following because unless that person has enough mettā and uh enough ability to teach there would not be many monks who would want to follow him a man of good family that means to be born into a good family a good family is not necessarily a rich family good family is one which is upright who knows the Dhamma who follows the the Dhamma straightforward simple people it's not easy to find sometimes a flare a fair content with little uh somebody who has few a few wishes a few things to ask for and freedom from sickness this is another great blessing to be seldom signal now the next two ties one uh.

sutta: Monks if even for the time of a finger snap a monk develops the first jhāna he is called a monk who does not dwell devoid of jhāna he carries out the master's instruction he responds to advice and he does not eat the country's alms food in vain how much more then those who make much of it.
commentry: In this sutta the Buddha is praising among who can attain jhāna even for a short very short time as as short as a finger snap so here firstly we find that the Buddha praises jhāna praises the attainment of chana and this is quite different from uh nowadays some people not belittle jaana the other thing we can see from this is that jhāna can be attained even for a short moment even a person they how long a person can attain jhāna and abide in ghana depends on his how long has cultivated it cultivated in a person is new has just attained jhāna it's not very stable and he could attain it for a short time but with longer practice perhaps he could he can abide in china for longer and longer this jhāna I just like to explain a bit this word jhāna is sometimes translated differently years ago they used to translate it as trans musing etc and more recently they translated it as absorption and mental absorption but the word jhāna literally means incandescence bright brightness so probably translation which is nearer the root word now would be a state of mental incandescence because a person who can attain jhāna his mind becomes bright that's why when it's reborn into the jhāna plane it becomes a being with a lot of brightness now we go to the next suta.

sutta: Monks those who do not partake of mindfulness of the body do not partake of the deathless monks those who partake of mindfulness of the body partake of the deathless.
commentry: So here the Buddha is saying that mindfulness of the body is extremely important it can bring us to the deathless state but here it uh it is my personal opinion that body here includes the mind also body sometimes because there's some other uh where the Buddha mentions the body with the perceptions and mental workings so here even though it doesn't mention perception and mental workings I would assume that it includes the mental partner the mind because the body and the mind are the two compo two things that we associate with the cell the Buddha said uh we always associate the cell with the five kandas and the five kandas if you analyze it now it is actually the body and full mental parts which constitute the mind.
